epigraph.text: I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith.

content.text: A curve through space has a length, even if it twists and turns. In this section, we develop formulas for measuring **arc length**—the actual distance along a curve—and **curvature**—how sharply a curve bends at each point. These concepts are fundamental to understanding the geometry of curves in space.
We derived the arc length formula for plane curves in Volume 2. The extension to space curves is natural.

content.title: Arc Length Formula
content.content:
If a curve $C$ is traced by a smooth vector function $\vect{r}(t) = \langle f(t), g(t), h(t) \rangle$ for $a \leq t \leq b$, then the **arc length** of $C$ is
\[
 L = \int_a^b |\vect{r}'(t)|\, dt = \int_a^b \sqrt{[f'(t)]^2 + [g'(t)]^2 + [h'(t)]^2}\, dt.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text: The quantity $|\vect{r}'(t)|$ represents the **speed** at which the curve is traversed. Arc length is speed integrated over time.

content.title: Arc length of a helix
content.problem: Find the length of one turn of the helix $\vect{r}(t) = \langle 2\cos t, 2\sin t, 3t \rangle$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 2\pi$.
content.solution:
First, find $\vect{r}'(t)$:
\[
 \vect{r}'(t) = \langle -2\sin t, 2\cos t, 3 \rangle.
\]
The speed is:
\[
 |\vect{r}'(t)| = \sqrt{4\sin^2 t + 4\cos^2 t + 9} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13}.
\]
Since the speed is constant, the arc length is:
\[
 L = \int_0^{2\pi} \sqrt{13}\, dt = \sqrt{13} \cdot 2\pi = 2\pi\sqrt{13}.
\]

content.title: Arc length of a twisted cubic
content.problem: Set up (but do not evaluate) an integral for the arc length of $\vect{r}(t) = \langle t, t^2, t^3 \rangle$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 1$.
content.solution:
We have $\vect{r}'(t) = \langle 1, 2t, 3t^2 \rangle$, so:
\[
 |\vect{r}'(t)| = \sqrt{1 + 4t^2 + 9t^4}.
\]
The arc length is:
\[
 L = \int_0^1 \sqrt{1 + 4t^2 + 9t^4}\, dt.
\]
This integral cannot be evaluated in terms of elementary functions.

content.text: A particularly natural way to parametrize a curve is by its arc length.

content.title: Arc Length Function
content.content:
If $C$ is a smooth curve defined by $\vect{r}(t)$ for $t \geq a$, the **arc length function** is
\[
 s(t) = \int_a^t |\vect{r}'(u)|\, du.
\]
This gives the length of the curve from $\vect{r}(a)$ to $\vect{r}(t)$.

content.text:
By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:
\[
 \frac{ds}{dt} = |\vect{r}'(t)|.
\]

content.title: Arc Length Parametrization
content.content: A curve $\vect{r}(s)$ is parametrized by **arc length** if $\left|\dfrac{d\vect{r}}{ds}\right| = 1$ for all $s$.

content.text: In an arc length parametrization, the parameter $s$ represents actual distance along the curve, and the curve is traversed at unit speed.

content.title: Reparametrizing by arc length
content.problem: Reparametrize the helix $\vect{r}(t) = \langle 3\cos t, 3\sin t, 4t \rangle$ with respect to arc length measured from $(3, 0, 0)$.
content.solution:
First, compute speed:
\[
 \vect{r}'(t) = \langle -3\sin t, 3\cos t, 4 \rangle, \quad |\vect{r}'(t)| = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5.
\]
The arc length function (from $t = 0$) is:
\[
 s(t) = \int_0^t 5\, du = 5t.
\]
Solving for $t$: $t = s/5$.

Substituting into $\vect{r}$:
\[
 \vect{r}(s) = \left\langle 3\cos\frac{s}{5}, 3\sin\frac{s}{5}, \frac{4s}{5} \right\rangle.
\]
We can verify: $|d\vect{r}/ds| = \frac{1}{5}|\vect{r}'(t)| = \frac{1}{5}(5) = 1$. $\checkmark$

content.text: Curvature measures how sharply a curve bends. A straight line has zero curvature; a tight turn has high curvature.

content.title: Curvature
content.content:
The **curvature** of a curve at a point is
\[
 \kappa = \left|\frac{d\vect{T}}{ds}\right|,
\]
where $\vect{T}$ is the unit tangent vector and $s$ is arc length.

content.text: Curvature measures the rate at which the tangent direction changes with respect to distance traveled. The faster the direction changes, the more curved the path.

content.title: Curvature Formulas
content.content:
If $\vect{r}(t)$ is a smooth curve with $\vect{r}'(t) \neq \vect{0}$, then
\[
 \kappa = \frac{|\vect{T}'(t)|}{|\vect{r}'(t)|} = \frac{|\vect{r}'(t) \crossp \vect{r}''(t)|}{|\vect{r}'(t)|^3}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
By the chain rule:
\[
 \frac{d\vect{T}}{ds} = \frac{d\vect{T}}{dt} \cdot \frac{dt}{ds} = \frac{\vect{T}'(t)}{|\vect{r}'(t)|}.
\]
For the cross product formula, note that $\vect{r}' = |\vect{r}'|\vect{T}$, so:
\[
 \vect{r}'' = \frac{d}{dt}(|\vect{r}'|\vect{T}) = \frac{d|\vect{r}'|}{dt}\vect{T} + |\vect{r}'|\vect{T}'.
\]
Then:
\[
 \vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}'' = |\vect{r}'|\vect{T} \crossp \left(\frac{d|\vect{r}'|}{dt}\vect{T} + |\vect{r}'|\vect{T}'\right) = |\vect{r}'|^2 (\vect{T} \crossp \vect{T}'),
\]
since $\vect{T} \crossp \vect{T} = \vect{0}$.

Taking magnitudes and using $|\vect{T} \crossp \vect{T}'| = |\vect{T}||\vect{T}'|\sin(90°) = |\vect{T}'|$ (since $\vect{T} \perp \vect{T}'$):
\[
 |\vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}''| = |\vect{r}'|^2 |\vect{T}'|.
\]
Thus $\kappa = \dfrac{|\vect{T}'|}{|\vect{r}'|} = \dfrac{|\vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}''|}{|\vect{r}'|^3}$.

content.title: Curvature of a circle
content.problem: Find the curvature of a circle of radius $a$.
content.solution:
Parametrize: $\vect{r}(t) = \langle a\cos t, a\sin t \rangle$.

Then $\vect{r}'(t) = \langle -a\sin t, a\cos t \rangle$ and $|\vect{r}'| = a$.

The unit tangent is $\vect{T}(t) = \langle -\sin t, \cos t \rangle$.

So $\vect{T}'(t) = \langle -\cos t, -\sin t \rangle$ and $|\vect{T}'| = 1$.

Thus $\kappa = \dfrac{|\vect{T}'|}{|\vect{r}'|} = \dfrac{1}{a}$.

The curvature of a circle is the reciprocal of its radius—constant around the entire circle.

content.title: Curvature of a helix
content.problem: Find the curvature of the helix $\vect{r}(t) = \langle a\cos t, a\sin t, bt \rangle$.
content.solution:
$$
 \vect{r}'(t) &= \langle -a\sin t, a\cos t, b \rangle, \\
 \vect{r}''(t) &= \langle -a\cos t, -a\sin t, 0 \rangle.
$$
The cross product:
\[
 \vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}'' = \begin{vmatrix} \vecti & \vectj & \vectk \\ -a\sin t & a\cos t & b \\ -a\cos t & -a\sin t & 0 \end{vmatrix} = \langle ab\sin t, -ab\cos t, a^2 \rangle.
\]
Magnitudes:
\[
 |\vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}''| = \sqrt{a^2b^2 + a^4} = a\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}, \quad |\vect{r}'| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}.
\]
Curvature:
\[
 \kappa = \frac{a\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}{(a^2 + b^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{a}{a^2 + b^2}.
\]
This is constant. When $b = 0$ (a circle), $\kappa = 1/a$ as expected.

content.text: For plane curves $y = f(x)$, there is a simpler formula:

content.title: Curvature of a Plane Curve
content.content:
If $y = f(x)$ is twice differentiable, then
\[
 \kappa = \frac{|f''(x)|}{[1 + (f'(x))^2]^{3/2}}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Parametrize the curve as $\vect{r}(t) = \langle t, f(t), 0 \rangle$ (using $x = t$ as parameter). Then:
$$
 \vect{r}'(t) &= \langle 1, f'(t), 0 \rangle, \\
 \vect{r}''(t) &= \langle 0, f''(t), 0 \rangle.
$$
The cross product is:
\[
 \vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}'' = \begin{vmatrix} \vecti & \vectj & \vectk \\ 1 & f' & 0 \\ 0 & f'' & 0 \end{vmatrix} = \langle 0, 0, f'' \rangle.
\]
Therefore $|\vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}''| = |f''|$ and $|\vect{r}'| = \sqrt{1 + (f')^2}$.

Using the curvature formula:
\[
 \kappa = \frac{|\vect{r}' \crossp \vect{r}''|}{|\vect{r}'|^3} = \frac{|f''|}{[1 + (f')^2]^{3/2}}.
\]

content.title: Curvature of an exponential curve
content.problem: Find the curvature of $y = e^x$ and determine where it is maximum.
content.solution:
We have $f(x) = e^x$, $f'(x) = e^x$, and $f''(x) = e^x$.

The curvature is:
\[
 \kappa(x) = \frac{|e^x|}{[1 + e^{2x}]^{3/2}} = \frac{e^x}{(1 + e^{2x})^{3/2}}.
\]

To find the maximum, we differentiate $\kappa$ with respect to $x$. Using the quotient rule:
\[
 \frac{d\kappa}{dx} = \frac{e^x(1 + e^{2x})^{3/2} - e^x \cdot \frac{3}{2}(1 + e^{2x})^{1/2} \cdot 2e^{2x}}{(1 + e^{2x})^3}.
\]

Setting the numerator equal to zero:
\[
 (1 + e^{2x})^{1/2} \left[ (1 + e^{2x}) - 3e^{2x} \right] = 0
\]
\[
 1 + e^{2x} - 3e^{2x} = 0 \implies 1 - 2e^{2x} = 0 \implies e^{2x} = \frac{1}{2}.
\]

Thus $2x = \ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$, so $x = -\frac{\ln 2}{2}$.

At this point, $e^x = e^{-(\ln 2)/2} = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and $e^{2x} = 1/2$.

The maximum curvature is:
\[
 \kappa_{\max} = \frac{1/\sqrt{2}}{(1 + 1/2)^{3/2}} = \frac{1/\sqrt{2}}{(3/2)^{3/2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{2^{3/2}}{3^{3/2}} = \frac{2}{3\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{9}.
\]

content.title: Arc length of a parabola
content.problem: Find the arc length of $y = x^2$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 1$.
content.solution:
We parametrize as $\vect{r}(x) = \langle x, x^2 \rangle$. Then $\vect{r}'(x) = \langle 1, 2x \rangle$ and
\[
 |\vect{r}'(x)| = \sqrt{1 + 4x^2}.
\]

The arc length is:
\[
 L = \int_0^1 \sqrt{1 + 4x^2}\, dx.
\]

This integral requires a trigonometric substitution. Let $2x = \tan\theta$, so $dx = \frac{1}{2}\sec^2\theta\,d\theta$.

When $x = 0$, $\theta = 0$; when $x = 1$, $\tan\theta = 2$.
$$
 L &= \int_0^{\arctan 2} \sqrt{1 + \tan^2\theta} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\sec^2\theta\,d\theta \\
 &= \frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\arctan 2} \sec^3\theta\,d\theta.
$$

Using the standard formula $\displaystyle\int \sec^3\theta\,d\theta = \frac{1}{2}[\sec\theta\tan\theta + \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta|] + C$:
\[
 L = \frac{1}{4}\left[\sec\theta\tan\theta + \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta|\right]_0^{\arctan 2}.
\]

At $\theta = \arctan 2$: $\tan\theta = 2$ and $\sec\theta = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5}$.
\[
 L = \frac{1}{4}\left[2\sqrt{5} + \ln(\sqrt{5} + 2) - 0 - 0\right] = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} + \frac{\ln(\sqrt{5} + 2)}{4} \approx 1.479.
\]

content.text: Curvature has a beautiful geometric interpretation. At any point on a curve:

items: **Curvature measures turning rate.** The quantity $\kappa = |d\vect{T}/ds|$ tells us how fast the direction of the curve is changing per unit distance traveled. A straight line has $\kappa = 0$; a tight turn has large $\kappa$.

**The osculating circle is the best-fitting circle.** At each point, there is a unique circle that:
 enumerate
has the same tangent direction, and

has the same curvature.
 enumerate
 This is the **osculating circle** (from the Latin *osculare*, "to kiss"). Its radius is $\rho = 1/\kappa$.

**Curvature determines acceleration.** For a particle moving at constant speed $v$, the normal (centripetal) acceleration is $a_N = \kappa v^2$. Higher curvature means more acceleration needed to stay on the path.

content.title: Comparing curvatures
content.problem: Compare the curvatures of circles with radii 1, 2, and 10 meters. What does this tell us about driving around curves?
content.solution:
For a circle of radius $R$, the curvature is $\kappa = 1/R$.

| Radius (m) | Curvature $\kappa$ (m$^{-1}$) | Centripetal accel. at 20 m/s |
| 1 | 1.000 | 400 m/s$^2$ $\approx 41g$ |
| 2 | 0.500 | 200 m/s$^2$ $\approx 20g$ |
| 10 | 0.100 | 40 m/s$^2$ $\approx 4g$ |

At highway speed (20 m/s $\approx$ 45 mph), a curve with 10-meter radius requires $4g$ of lateral acceleration—far beyond what tires can provide on a flat road. This is why highway curves have much larger radii (typically 200–500 m) and are banked to help provide the centripetal force.

The curvature formula $a_N = \kappa v^2$ shows that doubling speed quadruples the required centripetal force, which is why speed limits are lower on curvy roads.

content.title: Christiaan Huygens and the Cycloid
content.content: The Dutch mathematician Christiaan Huygens (1629–1695) studied curvature in connection with pendulum clocks. He discovered that a pendulum swinging in a cycloidal arc (rather than a circular arc) has a period independent of amplitude—making for more accurate timekeeping. The evolute of a cycloid is another cycloid, which Huygens used to design pendulum guides. His work on curvature laid foundations for differential geometry.

content.text:
The **radius of curvature** is the reciprocal of curvature:
\[
 \rho = \frac{1}{\kappa}.
\]

At each point of a curve, the **osculating circle** ("kissing circle") is the circle that best approximates the curve at that point. It has radius $\rho$ and lies in the plane of the curve's local bending.

content.title: Radius of curvature
content.problem: Find the radius of curvature for $y = x^2$ at the origin.
content.solution:
We have $f(x) = x^2$, so $f'(x) = 2x$ and $f''(x) = 2$.

At $x = 0$: $f'(0) = 0$, $f''(0) = 2$.
\[
 \kappa = \frac{|2|}{[1 + 0]^{3/2}} = 2.
\]
The radius of curvature is $\rho = 1/2$.

The parabola is best approximated at the origin by a circle of radius $1/2$ centered at $(0, 1/2)$.

marginNotes.text: In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths.
I will go before you and level the exalted places, I will break in pieces the doors of bronze and cut through the bars of iron.
The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.
It is he who sits above the circle of the earth, and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers.

marginNotes.author: Euclid
marginNotes.text: The laws of nature are but the mathematical thoughts of God.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text: Mathematics is the queen of the sciences and number theory is the queen of mathematics.
